headline: Singapore charged a fourth in the Nvidia chip case. No Malaysian operator faces charges yet.
context: Singapore charged three men in Feb 2025 over servers that, per the Home Affairs Minister, possibly carried Nvidia AI chips routed to Malaysia. A fourth was charged in April 2026. Malaysia imposed a Strategic Trade Permit on US AI chips on 14 July 2025. No Malaysian operator has been publicly named.

cards.big: Singapore's chip case grew to four people charged: three on 27 Feb 2025 (two Singaporeans and a Chinese national), a fourth on 2 April 2026.
cards.sub: Singapore's Minister Shanmugam said the servers possibly carried Nvidia chips and moved to Malaysia; the final destination was unknown.

cards.big: The US 'Tier 2' label came from a rule that never officially named Malaysia. BIS suspended enforcement before its main compliance date.
cards.sub: BIS issued the rule effective 13 Jan 2025, announced rescission 13 May 2025. GAO ruled in May 2026 the rule still stood until rulemaking completes.

cards.big: Johor approved 51 data-centre projects worth RM183 billion; 17 operational. In November 2025 the state stopped approving lower-tier DCs.
cards.sub: Residents protested at a Johor DC site in February 2026, the first such protest in Malaysia. YTL, ByteDance and GDS have Johor footprints.

cards.big: Malaysia made US-origin AI chips subject to a Strategic Trade Permit on 14 July 2025. Before that, MITI said the chips were not on the Strategic Items List.
cards.sub: Penalties under the July 2025 directive reach RM10M (RM20M with knowledge). No Malaysian operator faces public charges.

cards.big: Fifteen months on, the permit exists, Bridge swapped a tenant, Johor tightened approvals. No Malaysian operator has been publicly named.
Like writing the building code after the towers go up. Now everyone watches which ones get inspected.
cards.sub: Malaysia wrote its US-chip permit roughly five months after the Singapore charges. Bridge Data Centres reportedly swapped a Chinese tenant in February 2026.

cards.big: Singapore charged four. Malaysia wrote a permit. Bridge swapped a tenant. Fifteen months later, no Malaysian operator has been publicly named.
